evidence: Clear five-layer dependency direction: model → config → query → exploration → api. No upward references observed from lower layers into api.
collections/LongIntHashMap has zero cross-package dependencies — properly isolated utility.
exploration package imports from query (QueryExecutor, QueryResults) and model — both legitimate downward dependencies.

api package imports exploration types Edge and RelationEdgeType for JSON serialization in ExploreResponseBuilder. These graph types live in exploration rather than model, meaning the api layer reaches into an internal exploration sub-concern.

RelationUtils in api owns the alias-to-grammar-config-ID mapping (RELATION_ALIASES map). This knowledge could alternatively live in GrammarConfig/config layer but is primarily an HTTP URL parameter concern, so placement is defensible.

SemanticFieldExplorer contains a static FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT constant ('[xpos="NN.*"]') that duplicates the hardcoded-POS pattern style. This is only reached when GrammarConfig is null (test constructor), not in production paths.
No circular imports or hub modules found. WordSketchApiServer wires everything at startup and is the single composition root.
indexer/blacklab is correctly isolated; nothing outside Main.java references the indexer package.

Nine packages map cleanly to domain capabilities: model (data), config (grammar loading), query (corpus search), exploration (semantic field algorithms), api (HTTP handlers), collections (data structures), utils (math/CQL helpers), indexer/blacklab (corpus indexing), viz (SVG output).
model/package-info.java documents the naming convention split between traditional getX accessors and record-style accessors — a small but clear signal of intentional ownership.
ExploreResponseBuilder, ExplorationHandlers, and SketchHandlers each have a single, nameable responsibility that a new engineer can describe in one sentence.
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc lists all endpoints inline — the entry point is self-documenting.

Edge and RelationEdgeType are placed in the exploration package despite being pure presentation types (graph serialization) consumed only by the api layer — this blurs the exploration package's role as a 'computation' package.

SemanticFieldExplorer hosts both single-seed exploration logic and multi-seed exploration logic in one 370-line class; the multi-seed path (exploreMultiSeed) is architecturally a separate operation that shares no phase helpers with the single-seed path.

ComparisonResult is a traditional class (non-record) with mutable-style accessors while peer model types (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun) are records — minor inconsistency documented in package-info but visible to new contributors.
The ExploreOptions Javadoc explicitly calls out the Explore vs Exploration naming asymmetry as intentional — honest but the asymmetry itself is still a minor readability tax.
QueryExecutor interface has 7 methods and one production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor). However, it is an adapter isolating the external BlackLab library — a justified one-impl interface.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles delegates entirely to comparator.compareCollocateProfiles — a pure pass-through. The method exists to keep the public explorer API stable, but the delegation adds an indirection hop with no policy.
ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges is a single-purpose static utility that does real translation work — the abstraction pays its cost.
HttpApiUtils is a tightly scoped static utility for HTTP mechanics (CORS, JSON response, param parsing) — well-bounded, not a dumping ground.

GrammarConfig.toJson() embeds JSON serialization logic inside the data object. The method uses fastjson2 JSONObject construction directly in the model class, mixing persistence/presentation concerns with data.

RelationConfig.collocatePosGroup() performs inline string scanning across the pattern field rather than operating on a structured intermediate representation. This is abstraction bypass: callers depend on a parsed POS group but the Config record exposes raw pattern scanning each call.

ExploreOptions bundles 4 parameters (topCollocates, nounsPerSeed, minLogDice, minShared). nounsPerSeed is documented as inapplicable to multi-seed paths — the single bag is slightly overfitted to single-seed, with dead weight for multi-seed callers.

The SemanticFieldExplorer null-GrammarConfig constructor (for testing) means CollocateProfileComparator also accepts null GrammarConfig, creating two parallel code paths (config-derived vs. hardcoded fallback). The fallback abstraction is never exercised in production but still exists as maintained code.

pom.xml declares: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser (INL BlackLab), fastjson2 (2.0.57), slf4j-api (2.0.7), logback-classic (1.5.18), jspecify (1.0.0), junit-jupiter-api/engine, junit-platform-launcher.
Exactly one JSON library in use (fastjson2 2.0.57). No competing JSON libraries (no Jackson, no Gson, no org.json).
Logging stack is the canonical SLF4J + Logback pair. No competing logging frameworks.
jspecify 1.0.0 is the current stable release of the JSpecify nullability annotation library — correctly pinned.
fastjson2 version is 2.0.57 per the task's improvement notes confirming it was upgraded. This is current as of the review period.
BlackLab (blacklab-engine + blacklab-query-parser) is a specialized corpus search library that is the core engine of the project; its use is fully justified.
No duplicate purpose observed: no moment+dayjs style duplication.
No heavy dependency used for a trivial purpose (e.g., Guava pulled in for one utility method).
The com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer is used directly from the JDK for the REST server — no embedded HTTP server library dependency (Jetty/Undertow) is introduced. This is intentionally lightweight.
Maven shade plugin is used to produce a fat JAR — appropriate for a standalone server deployment.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: slf4j-api version 2.0.7 was released in 2023; logback-classic 1.5.18 is current. These are not mismatches but the slf4j-api pin could be bumped to 2.0.16 to stay current. Minor version drift is the only gap preventing a near-perfect score.

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern decomposes into four named phase helpers (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) — each does one thing with a clear name.
Guard clause pattern is used consistently in ExplorationHandlers: early returns after null checks (if (seed == null) return;) prevent deep nesting.
LongIntHashMap is a textbook open-addressing implementation — rehash, put, get, containsKey are all precisely bounded and direct.
LogDiceCalculator handles the dice<=0 edge case and NaN explicitly rather than letting floating-point oddities propagate silently.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens uses depth-counting bracket matching — the algorithm is correctly bounded and handles nested brackets and quoted strings.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern: 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(seed); }' — when seed is null, null is passed to ExplorationResult.empty() whose Javadoc says @param seed 'must not be null'. This is a minor contract violation in the guard clause.

ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams catches NumberFormatException and passes e.getMessage() directly to sendError — this leaks internal Java exception text (e.g. 'For input string: "abc"') to the HTTP client rather than a clean user message.

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits iterates up to limit = min(maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, records.size()) and calls getTotalFrequency for every hit record, including duplicates of the same collocate lemma. Corpus frequency is re-fetched per hit rather than per unique collocate, making the inner loop O(hits) index lookups instead of O(unique collocates).

exploreMultiSeed in SemanticFieldExplorer builds CoreCollocate list by streaming all values of seedCollocateMap for each collocate to compute avgLd — this is an O(seeds * collocates) pass that could be O(collocates) with a running accumulator.

CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList constructs a fullScores map that copies every noun's score including zeros — caller (ExplorationHandlers.formatAdjectiveProfile) then reads the map to build a response. The zero entries are present but correct given the model contract.

ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges provides a clean, single-method handoff from model objects (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) to graph Edge lists — the translation boundary is explicit and lives in one obvious place.
buildExploreResponseBody is a shared private method used by both single-seed and multi-seed handlers, preventing duplication at the response-assembly seam.

buildBaseExploreResponse extracts the common explore response structure — parameters, status, relation_type — into a single factory, reducing the risk of divergence between the two exploration endpoints.
Error and lifecycle propagation at boundaries is consistent: IOException → HTTP 500, IllegalArgumentException → HTTP 400 in all four ExplorationHandlers methods.

The response-building seam is split between ExplorationHandlers (for seed_collocates, discovered_nouns, core_collocates keys) and ExploreResponseBuilder (for edges) — a single response is assembled across two classes in the same package, which is manageable but not self-evident.

handleSemanticFieldComparison builds its 'adjectives' list by calling formatAdjectiveProfile inline rather than sharing the method with ExploreResponseBuilder. The formatAdjectiveProfile helper lives in ExplorationHandlers, not the response builder.

ComparisonResult.getFullyShared(), getPartiallyShared(), and getSpecific() are computed fresh on every call by streaming the full adjective list; handleSemanticFieldComparison calls all three to populate count fields, meaning three full passes over the adjective list at the boundary.
ExplorationHandlers.parseBaseExploreRequest is a private helper that all four exploration handlers call as a first step — clean, eliminates the URI parsing duplication.

The ExploreOptions record bundles single-seed-only parameter nounsPerSeed alongside cross-path parameters, creating a slightly awkward handoff for the multi-seed caller that ignores it (the code defensively rejects nouns_per in multi-seed requests at the HTTP layer).

RelationEdgeType.toString() has an explicit intent comment explaining why it overrides toString() — the comment adds non-obvious rationale (prevent logging frameworks from emitting Java enum names), not a restatement of the code.
ExploreOptions Javadoc calls out the Explore vs Exploration naming asymmetry as intentional — this is contextual WHY documentation rather than AI boilerplate.
model/package-info.java documents the getX vs record accessor naming convention distinction — genuinely useful for new contributors.
LogDiceCalculator.compute has a constant UNCOMPUTABLE = Double.NaN with Javadoc explaining why NaN was chosen over a magic number like -1 — informative.

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits contains: 'logger.debug("No joint frequency found for collocate '{}' — logDice will be 0", collocateLemma);' inside the per-hit scoring loop. This debug log fires once per hit record where the collocate has no frequency, which in a corpus of millions of hits could produce millions of log entries at DEBUG level — nosy debug logging in a hot loop.

ExplorationResult.getSeed(), getDiscoveredNouns(), etc. all have @return JavaDoc that restate the obvious: '@return the seed word this result was built from; never null'. The 'never null' contract is the only useful information; the rest restates the method name.
Main.java emits 'System.out.println("====...")' startup banners at the top level — these are UI, not debug logs, but they bypass the logging framework entirely.
GrammarConfigLoader has Javadoc on private helper methods (readConfigFile, missingConfigException) that explain the logic via prose what the method name already conveys.
The comment '// safe: min ensures result <= maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR' in CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery explains a non-obvious narrowing cast — genuinely useful.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: The per-hit debug log in scoreHits and the accessor-restating @return comments are minor but real AI-hallmark patterns found across several files.

evidence: RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint has a 'tag= fallback supports legacy grammar files' comment explicitly documenting that the fallback is intentional for backward compatibility with older grammar configs not yet migrated to xpos=. This is not incomplete migration — it is deliberate compatibility.

GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys actively rejects the 'copulas' key with a clear error message explaining the migration: copulas must now be embedded in CQL patterns. This is a completed migration with a guardrail.
GrammarConfigLoader: 'Canonical field name is pattern; cql_pattern is no longer supported' is a comment indicating a completed migration from cql_pattern to pattern.
No TODO, FIXME, HACK, or deprecated-but-called symbols found across the reviewed files.
No @Deprecated annotations on any method or class that are still called by active code.

RelationConfig.getFullPattern still uses getX naming — the task description notes 'getFullPattern→buildPattern renamed across 6 callers' as a recent improvement, but the actual code retains getFullPattern. Either this rename was not applied to this snapshot, or the task description describes a planned improvement. The actual code is consistent — all callers use getFullPattern — so there is no partial migration state.
The codebase is entirely Java 21+ with records, sealed interfaces, and modern stream APIs. No mixed Java version patterns observed.
No mixed JS/TS or old/new API coexistence patterns — the server uses com.sun.net.httpserver throughout with no competing HTTP server framework.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: The tag= fallback code path is retained for 'legacy grammar files' but there is no evidence any such files still exist in the test suite or production configuration. The backward compatibility may be orphaned.

evidence: Directory profiles: api/6 files, collections/1, config/3, exploration/5, indexer/blacklab/2, model/7, query/4, utils/2, viz/1 — no directory exceeds 7 files, no flat-dir overload flagged.
api package (6 files, 1278 LOC) is the largest; all files are HTTP handlers or HTTP utilities — high cohesion within the package.

model package (7 files) holds AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, ExplorationResult, PosGroup, RelationType, QueryResults, package-info — all are domain data types. Cohesion is high.

exploration package holds SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator (computation), ExploreOptions (option bag), Edge, RelationEdgeType (graph DTOs). Two of five files (Edge, RelationEdgeType) are data-transfer types consumed only by api, not by any exploration computation class — import-affinity mismatch.
query package holds QueryExecutor (interface), BlackLabQueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser — all tightly coupled to the BlackLab query execution pipeline. Cohesion is high.
config package (3 files): GrammarConfig (data), GrammarConfigLoader (loader), RelationConfig (config record) — appropriately grouped.
collections/LongIntHashMap is a single file. At one file, the package is thin but the class is genuinely specialized and used by the indexer.
viz/RadialPlot is a single file that generates SVG output. It has no coupling into the api or exploration packages and is called only via SketchHandlers.handleVisualRadial — correct placement.
utils/CqlUtils and LogDiceCalculator are correctly placed shared utilities used by config, query, and exploration packages.
RelationUtils in api package resolves URL parameter aliases to grammar config IDs — it is primarily an HTTP-layer concern and belongs in api.
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: Edge and RelationEdgeType in exploration are consumed only by the api package, creating an import-affinity mismatch. Their placement in exploration blurs the package's computation identity.

evidence: LongIntHashMap.get() Javadoc: 'Returns 0 if the key is absent' — clearly stated and correctly implemented.
LogDiceCalculator.compute() declares UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel and Javadoc says 'callers should use Double.isNaN(double) to detect this state' — contract is honest about the special return case.
QueryExecutor.findCollocations Javadoc specifies 'cqlPattern must start with [ and is treated as a collocate constraint' — a documented format precondition.

ExplorationResult.empty(@param seed 'the seed word; must not be null') but SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern calls empty(seed) inside 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty())' — the null branch passes null to a method whose contract prohibits it.

RelationConfig.getFullPattern(String headword): Javadoc says '@return the substituted pattern, or the original pattern when headword is null/blank' but the first line is 'if (pattern == null) return null;' — the null return path for null pattern is not documented in the @return.

RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() throws IllegalStateException (unchecked) when relationType is empty or PosGroup is OTHER — the Javadoc says '@throws IllegalStateException if...' which is correct documentation for unchecked exceptions.

ComparisonResult.getFullyShared/getPartiallyShared/getSpecific return freshly computed lists each call. Their Javadoc does not indicate this cost, but the contract ('returns adjectives present in every seed noun') is accurate.

ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed Javadoc: 'not used in multi-seed exploration (exploreMultiSeed), which accepts its parameters directly' — the contract honestly documents the limited applicability of the field.

QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern Javadoc: '@param lemma The head lemma (already substituted into bcqlPattern)' — this is an unusual dual-parameter contract (lemma AND bcqlPattern already containing lemma) that could confuse callers about whether to substitute or not.

SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples calls 'relationConfig.getFullPattern(noun.toLowerCase(), adjective.toLowerCase())' — the Javadoc for getFullPattern(String, String) says it 'delegates to getFullPattern(String) for the head, then injects the collocate lemma' which is accurate.

ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks finally block: comment reads 'chunks is NOT incremented so callers can detect the truncated output' but chunks++ immediately follows and IS executed on the error path — comment directly contradicts the code

SemanticFieldExplorer.deriveNounCqlConstraint uses findFirst() on a grammar-config stream filtered by PosGroup.NOUN; the result is silently order-dependent — changing relation ordering in the config file changes the noun CQL pattern used for exploration without any indication
CollocateProfileComparator.deriveAdjectivePattern has the same findFirst() order-dependency for adjective patterns

BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition checks position < 1 as a guard, but every call site already ensures collocatePos > 0 before the call (CollocateQueryHelper.extractCollocateLemma); the guard is always-false for normal call paths

ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams always parses nounsPerSeed from params even when called from handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, which has already rejected the parameter with a 400; the parsed value is computed but never used in the multi-seed path

CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch boolean parameter withStoredHits: callers pass literal true/false with no explanation at call sites — Boolean trap requires reading the method to understand intent

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(String, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions): validates relationType.isEmpty() and PosGroup.OTHER before calling the overload, but the overload itself has no null-seed guard; the null check is only in the overload body, one level deeper than the entry-point validation

SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType: rel.relationType().orElseThrow(...) is called inside a branch that already filtered on rel.relationType().orElse(null) == relationType, making the orElseThrow always succeed — dead throw

org.jspecify @Nullable is used consistently on return types that can be null: BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, extractHeadword, extractPlainTextTokenAt; HttpApiUtils.requireParam; ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig; ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams
QueryExecutor interface uses Closeable and declares checked IOException on all methods — type contracts align with implementation

ExplorationResult.empty(String seed) Javadoc states '@param seed the seed word; must not be null' but the calling code in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern passes null explicitly: `if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(seed); }` — contract violated at the call site
GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() is annotated @Nullable, consistent with the constructor accepting null for configPath when parsing from a Reader
RelationConfig uses Optional<RelationType> for the relationType field, and callers consistently check isEmpty()/orElseThrow() before use

CollocateQueryHelper constructor receives BlackLabIndex as a parameter with no null annotation; in tests it is passed null and the field is used in methods that are overridden — an invisible null-safety contract

SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType calls rel.relationType().orElseThrow() inside a branch guarded by rel.relationType().orElse(null) == relationType — the Optional type does not capture the now-guaranteed non-empty state, so orElseThrow carries a misleading failure semantics
Map<String, Object> used extensively for HTTP response building — no typed response DTOs; type information is entirely lost at the serialization boundary

RelationConfig.getFullPattern(String) uses a JavaBeans-style get prefix on a method that constructs/builds a new CQL pattern string from a template — 'get' implies simple field access but the method calls CqlUtils.substituteAtPosition and creates a new String

SketchHandlers.iterateRelations is named like a passive iterator but actually executes queries, formats results, and accumulates them into a map — the name understates the work done; processRelations or executeAndAccumulateRelations would match behavior
CollocateQueryHelper boolean parameter withStoredHits in executeCollocateSearch is a boolean trap — callers pass true/false literals with no semantic label

ExploreOptions vs ExplorationResult naming asymmetry (Explore vs Exploration prefix) is acknowledged in the ExploreOptions Javadoc as historical — the comment explains the asymmetry, which is good, but the asymmetry still taxes a new reader
SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchSeedCollocates — 'fetch' is consistent with the executor vocabulary (findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern) and clearly names the intent
CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits / scoreHits / buildAndRankCollocates follow a consistent verb-noun naming pattern matching the pipeline phases
BlackLabQueryExecutor methods follow a clear vocabulary: find (corpus lookup), execute (query runner), get (simple retrieval)
LogDiceCalculator.UNCOMPUTABLE constant name clearly communicates the sentinel meaning better than NaN or INVALID
SketchHandlers.ExecutedSketch record name is an awkward past-participle compound — SketchQueryResult or RelationQueryResult would be clearer

HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams catches Exception during URL decoding and only logs a warning — the parameter is silently dropped from the map, which later manifests as a mysterious '400 Missing required parameter' with no indication that decoding failed

WordSketchApiServer.wrapHandler has two catch blocks: IOException -> 500, Exception -> 500; IllegalArgumentException from parameter validation would be caught by the generic Exception branch and return 500, but handlers like ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore already catch IllegalArgumentException locally and return 400 — same exception type maps to different HTTP codes depending on which handler was called

SketchHandlers.handleBcqlQueryPost has 4 catch blocks: IOException->500, JSONException|IllegalArgumentException->400, Exception->500 — this is the most granular handler and inconsistent with others that only have IOException->500 or no local catch at all
CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency wraps RuntimeException in IOException — consistent with QueryExecutor interface contract of declaring IOException, good wrapping pattern

SketchHandlers.iterateRelations catches IOException | RuntimeException per relation and accumulates in relationErrors rather than aborting — intentional boundary design, well-suited for partial results
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand: explicit IOException catch for grammarConfig load with immediate executor.close() and return — good cleanup boundary
GrammarConfigLoader: throws IOException with specific messages for each validation failure (missing version, duplicate id, missing pattern) — consistent and informative

ExplorationHandlers handlers consistently catch IOException->500 and IllegalArgumentException->400 — but this is only true for the exploration handlers; SketchHandlers does not have this pattern for most handlers

HttpApiUtils.CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN is a static final field computed at class-load time from System.getProperty('cors.allow.origin', 'http://localhost:3000') — any test that changes this property after HttpApiUtils is loaded will not see the change; the class must be reloaded for the property to take effect

WordSketchApiServer constructor creates a com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer and binds to the loopback port (side effect: socket allocation) before the constructor returns — if registerRoutes() throws, the port is already bound but the server object is not usable
SemanticFieldExplorer constructor: derives nounCqlConstraint from grammarConfig via pure computation, then creates CollocateProfileComparator — no I/O, no network calls, clean initialization
CollocateProfileComparator constructor: derives adjectivePattern from grammarConfig via pure computation — no side effects
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor opens the index from disk — I/O side effect in constructor; callers must handle IOException at construction time, not at first use

Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand: executor is created first, then grammarConfig is loaded — if grammarConfig fails, executor.close() is called explicitly; the ordering is correct and the cleanup is explicit
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish(): reads System.getProperty('grammar.config') at call time, not at class load — this is a getter pattern, not a static initializer, so it is testable
No circular initialization dependencies found: api layer depends on query/exploration layers; exploration depends on query; query depends on utils; no cycles
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: HttpApiUtils.CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN is frozen at class load from a system property, making it fragile in in-process test scenarios; WordSketchApiServer constructor has a port-binding side effect that can leave the system in a partially-initialized state on construction failure.

authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The /api/bcql endpoint accepts arbitrary corpus queries without query-complexity limits beyond string length; a maliciously crafted pattern could cause a resource-exhausting Lucene query.

iterateRelations uses a BiFunction callback to share the relation-iteration loop — appropriate abstraction that eliminates duplication between surface and dependency sketch paths
WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes is a flat list of handler registrations — clean, no nesting
The response-building helpers (formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates) are small, focused, and correctly extracted

strengths: Five-layer dependency DAG (model→config→query→exploration→api) is strict and consistently observed across all 34 source files.
WordSketchApiServer acts as a single composition root with no logic, keeping wiring concerns centralized.
indexer/blacklab is correctly isolated from the query and api layers; only Main.java references it.
collections/LongIntHashMap carries zero cross-package imports — a genuinely standalone utility.
No circular dependency cycles detected anywhere in the codebase.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are minor boundary-placement questions (graph types in exploration vs model) rather than structural violations or hub-module problems.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The architecture is well-executed for a project of this size — layer direction is enforced, the composition root is clean, and no cycles exist. The one real tension is that Edge and RelationEdgeType live in the exploration package yet are consumed by the api layer for pure JSON serialization; moving them to model would tighten the boundary. The FALLBACK constant in SemanticFieldExplorer is a minor code smell limited to the null-GrammarConfig test path. These are small improvements, not structural failures, placing the score clearly in the solid-but-uneven band.

strengths: Package names correspond precisely to domain capabilities — a new engineer can predict file location from the domain term alone.
WordSketchApiServer is a pure wiring class with endpoint documentation in its Javadoc; no business logic leaks into the composition root.
model/package-info.java proactively documents cross-cutting naming conventions, signaling intentional architecture stewardship.
ExploreResponseBuilder is a focused, named translator class that keeps model objects free of presentation concerns.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are minor ownership blurriness at specific boundaries (exploration package hosting presentation types; SemanticFieldExplorer hosting two algorithms) rather than systemic decomposition failure.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The high-level structure is domain-aligned and predictable, which earns a solid base. Two things prevent a higher score: Edge and RelationEdgeType live in exploration where they create conceptual noise for any engineer reading the exploration package as 'computation logic', and SemanticFieldExplorer bundles two independently-motivated algorithms (single-seed vs multi-seed) whose helpers do not overlap, making the class's stated responsibility 'analyse semantic fields' a mild abstraction over two distinct operations. Neither issue is a blocker, but they are the type of surprise that a new engineer would encounter on their first code-reading session.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is a textbook external-adapter abstraction — it isolates a complex third-party library (BlackLab) behind a clean, testable seam.
ExploreResponseBuilder is a focused translator with genuine logic (model-to-graph conversion), not a pass-through.
CqlUtils and LogDiceCalculator are small, coherent utility classes with no mixed concerns.
HttpApiUtils is a well-bounded static helper covering exactly the HTTP mechanics needed without becoming a catch-all.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Issues are localised: one pure-delegation pass-through (compareCollocateProfiles), one concern-mixing in a data object (GrammarConfig.toJson), and one inline computation that should be a derived property (collocatePosGroup pattern scanning).
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase earns credit for the disciplined QueryExecutor adapter and the focused utility classes. Two abstractions don't fully pay their cost: compareCollocateProfiles in SemanticFieldExplorer adds an indirection hop that delivers no policy, and GrammarConfig.toJson mixes serialization into a data object. RelationConfig.collocatePosGroup re-scans the raw pattern string on every call rather than parsing to a richer intermediate, which is a leaky abstraction (callers ask for the parsed group but the record has no parsed state). These are real but fixable issues, keeping the score in the functional-but-uneven range rather than the solid band.

strengths: Single JSON library (fastjson2 2.0.57) — no competing JSON library duplication.
Canonical SLF4J + Logback logging stack with no competing frameworks.
JDK HttpServer used instead of adding an embedded server dependency — the project uses zero HTTP framework deps.
jspecify 1.0.0 pinned at the current stable release.
Dependency set is minimal and purpose-driven: every dependency maps to a distinct, central concern (corpus search, JSON, logging, nullability, testing).
dependency_health.issue_character: There are no meaningful dependency health issues; the only possible finding is minor version drift on slf4j-api, which is cosmetic.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The project's dependency graph is among the cleanest possible for a Java project of this complexity: five runtime dependencies all serving distinct, non-overlapping purposes, no version conflicts, no duplicate-purpose libraries, and the fastjson2 library was already upgraded to 2.0.57. The score lands in the high-solid band rather than exemplary only because slf4j-api could be pinned more aggressively to the latest minor and there is no documented BOM for managing BlackLab version alignment explicitly.

strengths: Four-phase decomposition of exploreByPattern into named helper methods is a model of balanced extraction — none of the helpers is trivial or over-fragmented.
Guard clause pattern applied consistently across all four ExplorationHandlers methods avoids deep nesting.
LongIntHashMap implementation is precise and boundary-correct with no defensive sprawl.
CqlUtils bracket-depth parser correctly handles nested brackets and quoted strings without a regular expression.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are a mix of a minor contract violation (null seed passthrough), a UX leak (raw exception message in error response), and a computational redundancy in CollocateQueryHelper's hit-scoring loop — all fixable, none catastrophic.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Function internals are generally direct and intention-revealing, and the phase decomposition in SemanticFieldExplorer is a genuine craft highlight. The score is pulled into the mid-70s by three real issues: the null-seed guard clause violates its own contract, the NumberFormatException message leaks to clients, and the per-hit corpus frequency lookup in scoreHits is computationally redundant (fetching the same lemma's frequency once per hit rather than once per unique collocate). These are the kind of issues that would surface in code review and require a follow-up fix rather than a rewrite.

strengths: ExploreResponseBuilder provides a named, explicit translation seam for model-to-graph conversion — the boundary is one class, one method.
Error handling at all exploration handler boundaries is consistent: IOException→500, IllegalArgumentException→400, with no mixed strategies.
parseBaseExploreRequest shared preamble eliminates URI-parsing duplication across all four handlers.
buildBaseExploreResponse factories the common explore response structure, preventing parameter map divergence between single-seed and multi-seed paths.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are seam-splitting (response assembly across two classes) and minor boundary inefficiency (repeated streaming passes in ComparisonResult), rather than systemic glue-code entropy.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The integration seams are generally clean and predictable: named handoff methods, consistent error propagation, shared preamble helpers. The score is reduced by two real friction points: response assembly is split between ExplorationHandlers (fields) and ExploreResponseBuilder (edges) for the same HTTP response, requiring a reader to look in two places; and ComparisonResult's derived views (getFullyShared etc.) are called three times at the boundary without a caching layer, doing redundant stream passes. Neither issue is severe, but they are the type of 'where does this go?' confusion that costs developer time.

strengths: Comments throughout explain non-obvious intent: RelationEdgeType.toString() intent comment, UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel rationale, ExploreOptions naming asymmetry note.
No verbatim copy-paste error handling boilerplate — each handler has contextually specific error messages.
No pass-through wrapper functions with no added logic (QueryExecutor adapter methods are justified adapters, not AI boilerplate wrappers).
No generic names in domain code — method names like exploreByPattern, buildCollocateToNounsMap, identifyCoreCollocates use domain vocabulary.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: Issues are isolated to a debug log in a hot path and some accessor-restating @return Javadoc rather than systemic AI-boilerplate patterns.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase shows clear evidence of intentional, informative documentation — a high bar. The vast majority of comments add genuine insight. Two patterns bring the score down slightly: the per-hit debug log in CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits could produce severe log noise in production at DEBUG level (a hallmark of AI-generated 'log everything' instrumentation), and several @return Javadoc entries on simple accessors (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) restate the method name without adding the contract information that would justify the Javadoc presence. These are minor and localised, placing the score solidly in the good-but-not-exemplary band.

strengths: validateNoLegacyKeys in GrammarConfigLoader hard-fails on the deprecated 'copulas' key with a migration-guiding error message — the completed migration is enforced, not just hoped for.
The cql_pattern→pattern migration is completed and the old field name is explicitly rejected.
No @Deprecated symbols called by active code anywhere in the reviewed files.
The entire codebase uses a single HTTP server API (com.sun.net.httpserver) with no competing framework remnants.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The only potential issue is a retained tag= fallback code path whose active consumers may no longer exist — a possible orphaned compatibility shim rather than an in-flight migration.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase has successfully completed several migrations (copulas→CQL patterns, cql_pattern→pattern, hardcoded POS→GrammarConfig-derived) and has left guardrails where appropriate. The score lands just below exemplary because the tag= compatibility path in RelationConfig is retained without a test exercising a tag=-format grammar file, making it a possible dead compatibility shim. This is a minor concern, not a systemic incomplete-migration problem.

strengths: No directory exceeds 7 files — no flat-dir overload concern.
api, config, query, model packages each have high internal cohesion — all files share a clear domain concern.
indexer/blacklab is isolated in a sub-package, correctly separating corpus-building from corpus-querying concerns.
utils contains only genuinely shared primitive utilities (CQL parsing, logDice math) with no dumping-ground creep.
package_organization.issue_character: The primary issue is two presentation-layer types (Edge, RelationEdgeType) placed in the computation-oriented exploration package, creating an import-affinity mismatch with the api package that consumes them.
package_organization.score_rationale: The package layout is well-organized and navigable — a new engineer can find most things by reasoning from domain terms to package names. The score is kept from the 85+ range by the Edge/RelationEdgeType placement: these two files live in exploration but their only consumers are in api, and exploration computation classes (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) never instantiate them. This is the definition of an import-affinity mismatch. Moving them to model would align the exploration package cleanly as 'computation' and the model package as 'all domain data types'. The fix is a small two-file move.

strengths: LogDiceCalculator.compute() NaN sentinel is precisely documented with instructions for callers (use Double.isNaN) — exemplary contract documentation for a numeric edge case.
ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed explicitly documents its inapplicability to multi-seed paths — honest about scope.
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() correctly documents its throws clause for unchecked exceptions.

QueryExecutor interface has thorough per-method contracts distinguishing findCollocations, executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, and executeSurfacePattern from each other — important for an interface with several similar-looking methods.
contract_coherence.issue_character: Issues are specific: one contract violation (null seed to empty()), one undocumented null return path (getFullPattern null pattern), and one ambiguous dual-contract (executeSurfacePattern lemma+bcqlPattern).
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has strong contract documentation in the core utility and interface classes. Three specific gaps pull the score into the mid-70s: ExplorationResult.empty() has a null-safety precondition that is violated by its primary caller; RelationConfig.getFullPattern has an undocumented null return for null patterns; and QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern carries an unusual dual-parameter contract (lemma is already substituted into bcqlPattern, yet both are required) that requires careful reading to understand correctly. None of these are critical bugs, but they are the type of contract surprises that produce head-scratching during debugging.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper phases (collect → score → rank) are cleanly separated into private methods with clear single-pass logic
SemanticFieldExplorer exploration phases (fetch seed collocates → build noun map → filter candidates → identify core collocates) are well-labeled and sequentially correct
ExplorationHandlers uses consistent guard-clause pattern: validate parameter → sendError and return null → proceed; control flow is easy to follow
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand properly sequences: open executor → load config → register shutdown hook → construct server, with explicit cleanup on config failure
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens bracket-depth tracking is correct for nested brackets and quoted strings
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are isolated rather than systemic: one misleading comment that contradicts observed behavior, two silently order-dependent findFirst() extractions, and one always-false guard clause — none of them indicate structural control-flow confusion.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase earns a solid rating because the vast majority of control flow does exactly what it claims: phases are clearly named, guards are consistent, and the execution model is linear. The ConlluConverter comment directly contradicts its code (a real clarity bug), and the findFirst() pattern for deriving CQL constraints creates silent fragility. These are localized issues that pull the score below the 'solid but uneven' anchor of 80.

strengths: @Nullable annotations from org.jspecify applied consistently on all public methods with nullable returns in BlackLabSnippetParser and HttpApiUtils
Optional<RelationType> used for the relationType relation config field, with consistent isEmpty()/orElseThrow() call patterns at use sites
QueryExecutor interface declares all exceptions (IOException), giving callers reliable type-level failure contracts
GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() correctly annotated @Nullable matching the constructor semantics
type_safety.issue_character: Issues are concentrated around two patterns: a null contract violation in ExplorationResult.empty() where the documented precondition is immediately broken by the only caller passing null, and untyped Map<String,Object> response objects that erase type safety at the serialization boundary.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase applies Java's available nullability tooling (jspecify @Nullable, Optional) consistently across the core API. The main deduction comes from the ExplorationResult.empty(null) contract breach, which is a correctness concern, and the pervasive use of Map<String,Object> for response construction that sacrifices type safety at the HTTP boundary. These are meaningful gaps but not systemic — most of the type surface is well-annotated.

strengths: Domain vocabulary is consistent: collocate, lemma, logDice, seed, relation, pattern — used with the same meaning across all layers
Phase-method names in CollocateQueryHelper (collectHits, scoreHits, buildAndRankCollocates) match the algorithm description in comments
LogDiceCalculator.UNCOMPUTABLE constant is named for its semantic meaning rather than its implementation detail (NaN)
BlackLabSnippetParser method names are specific and action-oriented: extractLemmaFromMatch, trimLeftAtSentenceBoundary, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition
naming_quality.issue_character: Issues are a mix of one systematic get-prefix misuse (getFullPattern across 6+ call sites), one misleading verb on a side-effecting method (iterateRelations), and one boolean-trap parameter — not a vocabulary collapse but a small set of recurring mismatches.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase has strong domain vocabulary consistency and clear method names in most modules. The deduction comes from getFullPattern which uses a get prefix for a method that constructs, not retrieves, and iterateRelations which names an execute-and-accumulate loop as if it were lazy iteration. These are meaningful because they create incorrect mental models for callers. The score sits between the 'solid but uneven' anchor at 80 and the 'significant drag' anchor at 60.

strengths: GrammarConfigLoader throws IOException with specific diagnostic messages for each config validation failure
CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency wraps RuntimeException in IOException to maintain the interface's checked-exception contract
SketchHandlers.iterateRelations intentionally accumulates relation-level errors rather than aborting — a well-designed partial-results boundary
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand correctly sequences cleanup on config load failure
error_consistency.issue_character: The inconsistency is structural: exploration handlers carefully map IllegalArgumentException to 400 and IOException to 500, but sketch handlers delegate all unhandled exceptions to wrapHandler which maps everything to 500; the two handler families have different error granularity with no documented rationale.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The domain layer has consistent IOException propagation and the config layer has good diagnostic errors. The API layer is split: ExplorationHandlers has deliberate exception-to-status mapping, SketchHandlers (except handleBcqlQueryPost) does not. The silent swallow in parseQueryParams is the most user-visible problem — a bad URL-encoded parameter causes a confusing downstream 400 rather than a clear decoding error. These combined issues place the score meaningfully below 70.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator constructors perform only pure config-derived computation — no I/O or network side effects
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand correctly sequences resource acquisition and cleanup: executor opened first, grammarConfig loaded second, with explicit cleanup path if loading fails
No circular initialization dependencies exist across the module hierarchy
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads the system property at call time rather than class-load time, making it override-friendly
initialization_coupling.issue_character: Issues are isolated: one static final computed from a system property at class load (HttpApiUtils.CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN) and one constructor with a socket-binding side effect (WordSketchApiServer) — both are common Java patterns but create friction in testing.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The initialization model is well-structured overall: constructors take dependencies as parameters, no module-level DB connections, and resource cleanup is explicit. The two deductions are both real: the static CORS property cannot be changed in-process after class load (a testing friction), and the WordSketchApiServer constructor binds a port before the object is fully initialized. Neither is severe enough to pull below 80, but both prevent reaching 90.

strengths: Package-info.java documents the mixed record/class convention in model/ with clear rationale — the asymmetry is intentional, not drift
All test classes follow the consistent SomeClassTest naming pattern
Handler classes consistently use package-private access with no public constructors, forming a coherent internal-API pattern
Consistent camelCase methods and PascalCase class names throughout — no snake_case islands
convention_outlier.issue_character: Issues are isolated to package-placement (RelationEdgeType in exploration/ vs model/) and access-modifier inconsistency (ExploreResponseBuilder public vs siblings package-private) — not a systemic naming drift but a pair of localized organizational outliers.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is well-organized with documented rationale for the one intentional mixed pattern (records vs classes in model/). The two deductions are the public ExploreResponseBuilder in an otherwise package-private api/ package, and the placement of RelationEdgeType in exploration/ when sibling enums live in model/. These are minor organizational mismatches that a new contributor would notice but could quickly learn to work with.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelperTest demonstrates the stub-override pattern that avoids needing a real BlackLab index — effective unit test design for a Lucene-backed class
CqlUtils and LogDiceCalculator have dedicated test classes covering their pure utility logic
GrammarConfigLoaderTest / RelationsValidationTest cover config loading with both valid and invalid inputs
SemanticFieldExplorerTest covers the explore success path with a mock executor
test_strategy.issue_character: Testing is strong for pure utility functions but thin for the HTTP handler layer (parameter validation edge cases in ExplorationHandlers) and for algorithmic edge cases in the exploration pipeline (deriveNounCqlConstraint order-dependency, ConlluConverter error paths).
test_strategy.score_rationale: The unit test suite for utility and core computation classes is solid. The gap is in the API handler layer — the most user-visible behavior — where parameter validation paths (multi-seed rejecting nouns_per, seeds < 2, unknown relation) and the implicit findFirst() order-dependency have no tests. Integration tests for the BlackLab backend require a live index, making them impractical in standard CI. This combination puts the score between 60 and 65.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface has a consistent (lemma, pattern, minLogDice, maxResults) parameter order across all 5 query methods
HTTP 'top' parameter standardized for max-results across concordance and exploration endpoints
ExploreOptions bundles tuning parameters into one record — avoids multi-argument creep for callers
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Issues concentrate on two boundaries: (a) parameter ordering inconsistencies at the package-private level (executeCollocateSearch reverses lemma/pattern, buildAndRankCollocates places an optional parameter mid-list), and (b) vocabulary drift for the max-results concept ('maxPerNoun', 'max' in HTTP vs 'top'/'topCollocates').
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The public QueryExecutor API is well-shaped with consistent conventions. Deductions come from internal API inconsistencies (CollocateQueryHelper parameter order and optional-mid-list placement) that create friction for contributors working in the query package, and the 'max' vs 'top' HTTP parameter inconsistency which affects API clients. Neither is severe but both reflect unconsolidated conventions across related surfaces.

strengths: Loopback-only binding provides consistent network-level access control for all endpoints without per-endpoint auth logic
CORS configuration is centralized in HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader() and applied consistently to every response
All endpoints are consistently unauthenticated, matching the tool's design as a local, single-user corpus research instrument
Read-only access pattern: no endpoint writes to the corpus, so there is no privileged operation that requires elevated access
authorization_consistency.issue_character: Authorization is not applicable in the traditional sense for this tool; the one operational concern is the BCQL endpoint's lack of query-complexity limits, which is a DoS risk rather than an authorization gap.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: For a local, read-only, single-user corpus search tool binding to loopback, the authorization model is correctly and consistently applied: everything is unauthenticated, all endpoints are equally open, and CORS is centralized. The small deduction is for the BCQL endpoint accepting arbitrary patterns without complexity budgeting. This is not an authorization failure but a resource-control gap that could be abused locally.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern phases are cleanly separated into named private helpers, each with a single algorithmic responsibility
CollocateQueryHelper three-phase BCQL pipeline (collect → score → rank) is correctly decomposed
iterateRelations uses a BiFunction callback to deduplicate the surface/dependency relation-iteration loops
Response-building helpers in ExplorationHandlers (formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates) are small and focused
design_coherence.issue_character: The main coherence issue is SketchHandlers accumulating responsibilities across very different domains (text queries vs SVG rendering vs arbitrary corpus queries), and ExplorationHandlers repeating a three-step validation preamble across three handlers that should share a request-pipeline abstraction.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The algorithmic core (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateQueryHelper) shows strong design coherence with well-extracted phases. The API layer is where coherence breaks down: SketchHandlers is a responsibility collector that has grown to include binary-SVG rendering alongside text queries, and the exploration handlers repeat validation boilerplate that would benefit from a shared request pipeline. These are real structural issues that make the file harder to navigate and extend, pulling the score to the upper end of the 60s.

issues.summary: Edge and RelationEdgeType live in exploration but are presentation-layer graph types used only by api
issues.suggestion: Move Edge and RelationEdgeType to the model package (alongside AdjectiveProfile, DiscoveredNoun, etc.) since they are data-transfer objects, then update ExploreResponseBuilder imports. This removes the api→exploration dependency for graph types and makes the exploration package contain only computation logic.
evidence: Edge and RelationEdgeType are created only in ExploreResponseBuilder (api package) and serialised to JSON; SemanticFieldExplorer never instantiates them.
Moving these types to model or api would remove the api→exploration import for graph construction while keeping exploration free of presentation concerns.

issues.summary: FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT in SemanticFieldExplorer hard-codes a POS pattern that bypasses GrammarConfig
issues.suggestion: Remove the null-GrammarConfig constructor overload in SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator, requiring tests to pass a minimal GrammarConfig fixture. This eliminates the parallel fallback path and ensures the config-derived pattern is the only code path.
evidence: private static final String FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]"; is used when grammarConfig is null.
The same pattern style was already migrated out of CollocateProfileComparator (FALLBACK_ADJECTIVE_PATTERN) but the dual null-constructor path keeps the fallback alive in both classes.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer hosts single-seed and multi-seed algorithms with no shared phase helpers
issues.suggestion: Extract exploreMultiSeed and its inline logic into a MultiSeedExplorer class in the exploration package, mirroring CollocateProfileComparator's extraction. SemanticFieldExplorer becomes a thin facade delegating to both, keeping the public API stable while making each algorithm's ownership clear.
evidence: exploreByPattern (single-seed) uses four phase helpers: fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates — none used by exploreMultiSeed.
exploreMultiSeed (multi-seed) performs its own per-seed collocate fetch, intersection, and CoreCollocate construction inline with no helper extraction.
The two paths differ architecturally: single-seed does a two-hop graph traversal; multi-seed does per-seed individual queries then intersects results.

issues.summary: Edge and RelationEdgeType belong to exploration but represent API-layer presentation concerns
issues.suggestion: Move Edge and RelationEdgeType to the model package, which already holds all other data-transfer types. Update imports in ExploreResponseBuilder and ExplorationHandlers (two callers).
evidence: Edge.toMap() serialises to JSON — it is a DTO, not a computation type.
Neither SemanticFieldExplorer nor CollocateProfileComparator instantiates Edge; only ExploreResponseBuilder in the api package does.

The exploration package now contains: computation (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator), option bags (ExploreOptions), and presentation DTOs (Edge, RelationEdgeType) — three different responsibilities.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles is a pure delegation with no added policy
issues.suggestion: Either expose CollocateProfileComparator directly to callers (ExplorationHandlers), or add a genuine responsibility to SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles (e.g., input validation, logging at the explorer level, or post-processing). A zero-addition delegation wrapper bloats the call graph without benefit.
evidence: public ComparisonResult compareCollocateProfiles(Set<String> seedNouns, double minLogDice, int maxPerNoun) throws IOException { return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seedNouns, minLogDice, maxPerNoun); }
The method signature, parameter names, and return type are identical to CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles. No validation, caching, logging, or transformation is added.

issues.summary: GrammarConfig.toJson() embeds fastjson2 serialization logic in a data object
issues.suggestion: Extract serialization to a GrammarConfigSerializer utility in the api or config package that accepts a GrammarConfig and returns JSONObject. This removes the fastjson2 import from config objects and allows changing the serialization format without touching the domain model.
evidence: GrammarConfig.toJson() directly constructs a JSONObject and JSONArray, coupling the model class to fastjson2.
RelationConfig.toJson() is similarly embedded in the record.
The serialization concern is only needed by SketchHandlers for the /api/relations endpoint; the config objects are used in many other places without needing JSON output.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.collocatePosGroup() re-scans the raw pattern string on every call with no parsed cache
issues.suggestion: Add collocatePosGroup as a computed field derived in a static factory or add it as a constructor parameter in RelationConfig so it is parsed once. Alternatively, cache it lazily inside a wrapper object. This makes the derived nature of PosGroup explicit in the type rather than hiding it in a method body.
evidence: collocatePosGroup() calls extractLabelContent() and posGroupFromConstraint() which scan the pattern string with string operations each invocation.
RelationConfig is a record (immutable), so the result of collocatePosGroup() is deterministic and could be computed once at construction time.
SemanticFieldExplorer.deriveNounCqlConstraint() calls collocatePosGroup() in a stream filter across all relations — repeated scanning for each element.

issues.summary: exploreByPattern passes null seed to ExplorationResult.empty() when seed is null, violating the @param contract
issues.suggestion: Change the guard to: 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(seed != null ? seed : ""); }' or split the null and empty checks so that null maps to an empty-string sentinel, or throw IllegalArgumentException for null seeds and document that callers must sanitise before calling.
evidence: Guard: 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(seed); }' passes null when seed is null.
ExplorationResult.empty(seed) Javadoc: '@param seed the seed word; must not be null'.

issues.summary: resolveExploreParams sends raw NumberFormatException.getMessage() to HTTP clients
issues.suggestion: Replace with a user-oriented message: 'Invalid numeric parameter: expected an integer for top/min_shared/nouns_per or a decimal for min_logdice'. Log the original exception at debug level for diagnostics.
evidence: catch (NumberFormatException e) { HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, "Invalid numeric parameter: " + e.getMessage()); }
NumberFormatException.getMessage() returns strings like 'For input string: "abc"' — a Java internal detail that leaks to the API client.

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits calls getTotalFrequency for every hit record, fetching the same collocate frequency multiple times
issues.suggestion: Build a collocateTotalFreqCache (Map<String,Long>) in the scoring phase: on first encounter of a collocate lemma, call getTotalFrequency and cache the result; on subsequent hits with the same lemma, read from the cache. This reduces index lookups from O(hits) to O(unique collocates).
evidence: In scoreHits: 'long collocateFreq = validCollocate ? getTotalFrequency(collocateLemma) : 0L;' is inside a loop that iterates up to maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR records.
Multiple records can share the same collocateLemma; each repeat hit for the same collocate triggers a redundant corpus index lookup.
collocateFreqMap already accumulates per-collocate joint frequencies in phase 1 — a similar accumulation for total corpus frequency could be done in the same pass.

issues.summary: Exploration response assembly is split across ExplorationHandlers (fields) and ExploreResponseBuilder (edges) for the same HTTP response
issues.suggestion: Move all ExplorationResult→response-map logic into ExploreResponseBuilder, making it the sole owner of the exploration-to-HTTP translation seam. ExplorationHandlers then calls ExploreResponseBuilder.buildExploreResponse(result) for the entire body, removing the split.
evidence: buildExploreResponseBody in ExplorationHandlers builds seed_collocates, discovered_nouns, core_collocates, then calls ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges for edges — the single response map is populated across two classes.

formatAdjectiveProfile in ExplorationHandlers serialises AdjectiveProfile to map; ExploreResponseBuilder serialises AdjectiveProfile to Edge — two classes each partially understand AdjectiveProfile serialisation.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison calls getFullyShared/getPartiallyShared/getSpecific producing three full streaming passes over the adjective list at the boundary
issues.suggestion: Add a ComparisonResult.getSummary() method returning a record with fullySharedCount, partiallySharedCount, and specificCount computed in a single pass, or cache the derived lists in ComparisonResult at construction time since it is immutable. The boundary caller then reads pre-computed values.
evidence: response.put("fully_shared_count", result.getFullyShared().size()); response.put("partially_shared_count", result.getPartiallyShared().size()); response.put("specific_count", result.getSpecific().size()); — three separate stream passes.
Each derived view re-streams the full adjective list with its own filter predicate.

issues.summary: logger.debug in CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits fires once per hit record with no-frequency collocate — potential log flood in hot path
